title: Risotto ai Funghi Porcini
subtitle: A classic Italian risotto — porcini-scented stock, Arborio rice, and Parmesan
description: Risotto requires attention but not complexity. The technique is the same throughout: add hot stock in ladlefuls, stir, wait for absorption, add more. The dried porcini in this version do two jobs — they go into the risotto itself and their soaking liquor perfumes the entire batch of stock. The result is a deeply savoury, earthy rice dish that needs nothing alongside it. The finishing step of mantecatura — beating cold butter and Parmesan into the finished rice off the heat — gives risotto its characteristic creamy, wave-like consistency.
sourceNotes: Risotto ai funghi porcini is a canonical dish of Northern Italian cuisine, particularly Lombardy and the Veneto. The mantecatura technique appears in Marcella Hazan's Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking (1992) and is standard in all credible Italian cookery sources.

recipeTools.notes: A wide, heavy-based saucepan or deep-sided sauté pan distributes heat evenly and allows sufficient surface area for the rice to cook without piling up.
For keeping the stock hot — cold stock slows the cooking process and affects the texture of the finished risotto.
For the constant stirring required during the absorption process.
For adding stock in consistent portions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The final stage of risotto-making: cold butter and Parmesan are beaten vigorously into the finished rice off the heat, creating the characteristic creamy, flowing consistency. The word means 'to cream' or 'to make silky'.
Wild cep mushrooms (Boletus edulis), dried and sold in small packets. The soaking water is as valuable as the mushrooms themselves — strain and use it as part of the stock.
The aromatic base of Italian cooking: onion (sometimes celery and carrot) cooked slowly in fat until soft and sweet. It forms the flavour foundation of most Italian sauces, stews, and risottos.

ingredients.notes: Soaked in 300 ml of boiling water for 20 minutes
Hot — combined with the strained porcini soaking liquor to make the risotto stock
Or Carnaroli — Carnaroli is more forgiving
30g for cooking, 30g cold for the mantecatura

steps: Soak the dried porcini in 300 ml of boiling water for 20 minutes. Lift out the mushrooms, squeeze gently, and chop roughly. Strain the soaking liquid through a fine sieve lined with kitchen paper. Combine the strained liquid with the vegetable stock in a medium saucepan and keep over a very low heat.
Sauté the chestnut mushrooms in 1 tbsp of olive oil in a frying pan over high heat for 4–5 minutes until golden. Season and set aside.

In a wide, heavy-based pan, warm the remaining olive oil with 30g of butter over a medium-low heat. Add the shallots and cook for 5–6 minutes until completely soft and translucent — this is the soffritto. Add the garlic for 1 minute.
Add the rice and stir for 2 minutes until each grain is coated in fat and looks translucent at the edges. This toasts the starch slightly and is the tostatura step.
Pour in the white wine and stir constantly until fully absorbed, about 2 minutes.

Add the chopped porcini. Then begin adding the hot stock one ladleful at a time, stirring continuously. Add the next ladle only when the previous one has been absorbed. Maintain a steady, gentle simmer throughout — if the heat is too low the rice stews; too high and the stock evaporates before the rice cooks.

After about 18–20 minutes the rice should be tender with a very slight resistance at the very centre (al dente). The consistency should flow slowly like lava when you tilt the pan — not stiff, not liquid.

Remove from the heat. Add the sautéed chestnut mushrooms, the remaining 30g of cold butter cut into cubes, and the Parmesan. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon for 1–2 minutes — this is the mantecatura. The risotto should become creamy and wave-like. Cover and rest for 2 minutes.
Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately in warm bowls with the parsley scattered over.

content.text: Two things determine a risotto's success: hot stock added in stages, and the

at the end. Cold stock shocks the rice and produces an uneven texture. The mantecatura (beating cold butter and Parmesan into the finished rice off the heat) transforms it from a thick stew into the glossy, flowing consistency that defines a proper risotto.

in 300 ml of boiling water for 20 minutes. Lift them out, squeeze, and chop roughly. Strain the soaking liquor through a fine sieve lined with kitchen paper to remove any grit. Combine with {{vegetable-stock}} of vegetable stock in a saucepan and keep over a very low heat.
Separately, sauté {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of chestnut mushrooms in a frying pan with 1 tbsp of olive oil over a high heat for 4–5 minutes until golden. Season and set aside.
Warm the remaining olive oil and 30g of butter in a wide, heavy pan over a medium-low heat. Add the shallots and cook for 5–6 minutes until completely soft — this is the
. Add the garlic for 1 minute. Add {{arborio-rice}} of rice and stir for 2 minutes until each grain is coated and translucent at the edges.

Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and stir until absorbed, about 2 minutes. Add the chopped porcini. Then add the hot stock one ladleful at a time, stirring continuously and waiting for each addition to be absorbed before adding the next. Keep the pan at a steady, gentle simmer throughout. After 18–20 minutes the rice should be tender with a slight resistance at its very core, and the whole mass should flow slowly when the pan is tilted.

Remove from the heat. Add the sautéed chestnut mushrooms, the remaining 30g of cold butter in cubes, and {{parmesan}} of grated Parmesan. Beat vigorously for 1–2 minutes — the risotto should turn glossy and creamy. Cover and rest for 2 minutes. Taste, adjust seasoning, and serve immediately in warm bowls with parsley scattered over.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Risotto has a narrow window — these are the most common problems.

attrs.solution: Stirred too vigorously for too long. The starch from the rice is what makes risotto creamy, but constant aggressive scraping breaks the grains and releases excessive starch. Stir steadily but not frantically, and stop once the stock is absorbed between each ladle.

attrs.problem: Risotto is stiff rather than flowing
attrs.solution: Not enough stock, or the rice has been cooked past the point of absorption. Add a final ladle of hot stock during the mantecatura and beat quickly to restore the flowing consistency. Serve immediately — risotto waits for no one.
